coredata.dc:title: One step synthesis of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite as a robust peroxidase- mimetic for instant unaided eye detection of glucose in serum, saliva and tear
coredata.dc:creator: Vinita,
Nirala, Narsingh R.
Prakash, Rajiv
coredata.dc:description: A graphene analogous of transition metal dichalcogenides, mainly molybdenum disulfide (MoS2), have recently attracted wide attention due to its catalytic and electrical properties. Herein, we report a simple one step synthesis of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) decorated over MoS2 quantum dots (AuNPs@MoS2-QDs) as a robust peroxidase- mimetic material. The AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite catalyzes the peroxidase substrate of 3, 3′, 5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) oxidation in the presence of H2O2 to produce a bluish-green colored product. The peroxidase mimetic behavior of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite complies with the steady state kinetics and is stable in harsh conditions such as high or low temperature and pH. The developed composite system has been used for sensitive and selective detection of glucose. The developed biosensor shows an excellent detection in dynamic ranges from 1 to 400 μM (R2∼0.996) with the detection limit of 0.068 μM in PBS buffer solution. Low Km value (0.06 mM) of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs has shown strong binding affinity towards substrates along with high values of V max (10.6 × 10−6 Ms−1) indicating greater catalytic reaction rate. Further the biosensor is developed as a portable test kit for detection of glucose in biological fluids like serum, tear and saliva utilizing agarose hydrogel as a visual detection platform. Our portable sensing kit shows an excellent response for detection of glucose in real samples compared to the conventional methods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: AuNPs@MoS2-QDs
Composite
Peroxidase
Glucose sensor
3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB)
Portable test kit

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Experimental procedure for preparation of MoS2-QDs
Optimization procedure of colorimetric sensing of glucose
Development of portable test kit for detection of diabetes in human using biological fluids (serum, saliva and tear)
Synthesis of nanomaterials and their characterizations
AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite catalysis the oxidation of TMB as the peroxidase substrates
Temperature, pH and H2O2 dependent AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite catalytic activity
Steady state kinetics of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite
H2O2 detection based on AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite system by colorimetric and electrochemical
Study of repeatability of biosensor system
Testing the glucose level through portable test kit in human biological fluids (serum, saliva and tear)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of MoS2-QDs and corresponding SAED pattern (b) particle size distribution of MoS2-QDs.
(a), (b) and (c) TEM image of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite (d) HR TEM image of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs showing lattice fringe spacing of AuNPs and MoS2-QDs Inset of figure (a) corresponding SAED pattern.
AFM image of MoS2 QDs and AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composites.

The absorbance spectra of TMB oxidation products for different reaction systems: (A) (a)AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite, (b) AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite +TMB, (Inset image shows enlarge view). (c) AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite + TMB + H2O2 and (B) time dependent study of (a) AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite, (b) TMB + H2O2 and (c)AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite + TMB + H2O2. (Inset image corresponding color image).The reaction system having AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite (0.2 μg/mL), TMB (1 mM) and H2O2 (0.15 mM).

Dependency of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite catalytic activity on (a) Temperature, (b) pH and (c) H2O2 concentration. Optimize reaction condition such as AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite (0.2 μg/ml), TMB (1 mM), H2O2 (0.15 mM), 1 ml of acetate buffer (0.2 M, pH 4.0) at 40 °C.

The enzyme kinetic parameter of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite system toward substrates (a and b). Optimized reaction condition of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite (0.2 μg/mL) in 1 ml of 0.2 M sodium acetate buffer at 40 °C (pH 4.0). (a) Kinetics of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite for H2O2 in fix TMB (0.1 mM) (b) kinetic for TMB in fix H2O2 concentration (1 mM).
The catalytic mechanism of TMB oxidation in presence H2O2 catalyzed by AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite.

(a) UV–vis spectra of colorimetric sensing of glucose based on AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite+ TMB + GOx in buffer. Inset shows visible color in presence of glucose (1, 2, 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 50, 100, 150, 200, 300 and 400 μM) (b) calibration curve.

(a) Interference study and (b) Selectivity of developed sensor system towards glucose. Reaction condition like 0.5 ml of sodium acetate buffer (0.2 M, pH 4.0) with TMB (1 mM), 0.2 μg/ml, AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite and 1 mg/ml, glucose oxidase with different interference.
The reproducibility test of developed sensing method (a) Intra-day and (b) Inter-day repeatability for same setup condition for glucose(1 μM to 400 μM) detection.

(a) UV–vis spectra of test of glucose level in serum by portable test kit (based on AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite) and inset change the color of wells hydrogel with presence glucose level, with typical colorimetric chart with level glucose (in order to 0, 2, 4, 5, 8, 10, 11, 12 mM), (top) and (b) calibration curve.

Absorption spectra of test of glucose level in tear by portable test kit and inset change the color of wells hydrogel with presence glucose level (50 μM, 100, 250, 400, 600, 800 and 1 mM) in tear (b) corresponding calibration curve.

Absorption spectra of test of glucose level in saliva by portable test kit and inset shows change in the color of wells hydrogel with presence glucose level (5 μM, 150, 250, 350, 450, 550, 700 and 800 μM) in saliva (b) corresponding calibration curve.
Comparison of various nanoparticles- based methods for detection of glucose.

head.ce:title: One step synthesis of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite as a robust peroxidase- mimetic for instant unaided eye detection of glucose in serum, saliva and tear

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology (BHU), Varanasi, 221005, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A graphene analogous of transition metal dichalcogenides, mainly molybdenum disulfide (MoS2), have recently attracted wide attention due to its catalytic and electrical properties. Herein, we report a simple one step synthesis of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) decorated over MoS2 quantum dots (AuNPs@MoS2-QDs) as a robust peroxidase- mimetic material. The AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite catalyzes the peroxidase substrate of 3, 3′, 5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) oxidation in the presence of H2O2 to produce a bluish-green colored product. The peroxidase mimetic behavior of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite complies with the steady state kinetics and is stable in harsh conditions such as high or low temperature and pH. The developed composite system has been used for sensitive and selective detection of glucose. The developed biosensor shows an excellent detection in dynamic ranges from 1 to 400 μM (R2∼0.996) with the detection limit of 0.068 μM in PBS buffer solution. Low Km value (0.06 mM) of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs has shown strong binding affinity towards substrates along with high values of V max (10.6 × 10−6 Ms−1) indicating greater catalytic reaction rate. Further the biosensor is developed as a portable test kit for detection of glucose in biological fluids like serum, tear and saliva utilizing agarose hydrogel as a visual detection platform. Our portable sensing kit shows an excellent response for detection of glucose in real samples compared to the conventional methods.

ce:section.ce:para: Nano enzymes have emerged as a significant tool for colorimetric detection with greater constancy and lower cost than protein enzymes. Due to their much higher stability, easy preparation, controllable structure, good tunability, catalytic activity and composition [1,2], nanozymes have attracted considerable attention in several applications such as nanomedicine, drug delivery, bio sensing, agriculture, and food processing [3–6]. Up to now, various types of nanomaterials such as gold nanoparticles [7], metal oxides [8–10], graphene quantum dots [11–13] etc. were exposed with peroxidase [14–16], oxidase [17], catalase [18], laccase [19] and superoxide dismutase mimicking activities [20]. Still, low cost, quick and sensitive colorimetric biosensors needs to be developed based on robust enzyme mimetic nano-enzymes.

Due to unique chemical and physical properties of new 2D nanomaterials, these are appearing as hot spot areas for scientists [21,22]. Among the 2D nanomaterials, research interest towards Molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) has been increasing due to it, having marvelous catalytic, electrical conductivity and biocompatibility properties [22,23]. New properties of 2D materials appear when transformed to zero-dimensional reason being prominent edge and quantum confinement effects [24]. In contrast to other MoS2 nanostructures, MoS2-QDs have higher surface area to volume ratio and better electron transport facility [25–27]. The remarkable properties of MoS2-QDs such as optical and electronic properties have been used in various applications from electronics (photo detectors and FETs etc.) to biomedical (bio-imaging and biosensors etc.) [24,28].

Recently, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) decorated inorganic semiconductors composite have enhanced catalytic activity and conductivity for development of highly sensitive biosensors [29–32]. Really, these materials have exciting properties like its optical and electronic properties which are originated from scarce hetero junctions and the exciton plasmon coupling [3,33]. Other nanocomposites mostly carbon derivatives require functionalization to establish chemical bonding with the gold nanoparticles [34]. In contrast, layered transition metal dichalcogenides, like WS2 and MoS2 which have sulphur atom over the outer layer are suitable for metal ligand formation that is, formation of strong AuS bonds. This chemical bonding can increase the stability of resulting nanocomposites and facilitate a charge transfer between sulphur and Au [29,35].

In the present work, firstly we have developed a simple and one step synthesis method of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite. The composite instantly catalyzed the peroxidase substrate 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine in presence of H2O2 to develop bluish-green colored products. Based on the investigation, AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite is used to detect H2O2 and glucose using colorimetric method. We developed sensing system with an excellent selectivity, reproducibility and stability towards glucose sensing. The proposed method was used to develop a portable test kit to detect glucose with unaided eye in real human fluid samples through agarose hydrogel being used as visual platform.

We used chemical reagents of analytical grade which are glucose, 30% hydrogen peroxide, horseradish peroxidase (HRP), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), Glucose oxidase (GOx, >100 units/mg), gold (III) tetrachloride trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O) and agarose, were purchased from sigma Aldrich (USA). Sodium molybdate dihydrate (Na2MoO4·2H2O) was bought from Moly Chem India (≥98% purity, Product code- 18600). l-cysteine, Hydrochloric acid (HCl), disodium hydrogen phosphate and sodium dihydrogen phosphate were purchased from Merck (Merck, India). We made all the chemical reagent solutions using ultrapure water. For the detection of glucose in human blood serum, the blood samples were collected from the authorized hospital.

XRD of synthesized MoS2-QDs and AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite were recorded by a Miniflex 600 X-ray-diffractometer (Cu–Kα radiation, Kα = 1.54056 Å, 3°/min scan rate) in the range between 5°–80°. Change in the absorbance was analyzed by using UV–vis Epoch 2 microplate reader Biotech (USA) spectrophotometer in quartz cuvette (1 cm optical path length). Structural characterization of composite materials was done by using HR-TEM, the Energy Dispersive X-ray and mapping by using FEI, TECHNAI G2 20 TWIN (Czech Republic) electron microscope operating with 200 keV accelerating voltage on a carbon-coated copper grid modified with 6 μL solution of the MoS2-QDs and AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite. Atomic force microscope (AFM) of MoS2-QDs and AuNPs@MoS2QDs were performed with NT-MDT, Russia on silicon wafer substrate. Cyclic voltammogram was recorded with Auto lab (PG STAT, 302, The Netherlands, NOVA 1.11 software) using a conventional three-electrode system. Zeta of MoS2-QDs and AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite were executed with Nanoparticles Analyzer SZ-100, Japan.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental procedure for preparation of MoS2-QDs
ce:section.ce:para: MoS2-QDs synthesis was performed through one step hydrothermal method. Individual solution of 0.5 g of l-cysteine (HO2CCH (NH2) CH2SH) and 0.25 g of sodium molybdate (Na2MoO4·2H2O) were prepared in 25 ml of distilled water (DI) with constant stirring for 15 min, then both the solutions were mixed followed by constant stirring at ∼40 °C and pH (3.0) maintained by concentrated HCl (12 N). Then to carry out hydrothermal process, the above solution was transferred into 100 ml capacity of Teflon autoclave (stainless steel lined) and left for 42 h at ∼200 °C. After the reaction, solution was left for cooling at room temperature to get yellow colloidal solution containing MoS2-QDs. The final product was dialyzed by dialysis bag (retained molecular weight 2000 Da) for 3–4 days to get the product of MoS2-QDs. (Fig. S1).

60 μL of MoS2-QDs was added to 5 ml aqueous solution of HAuCl4 (1 mM) at boiling along with vigorous stirring for 30 min. The appearance of light purple colored solution indicates the formation of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite formation.

For the colorimetric sensing of H2O2, 10 μL of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite (0.2 μg/ml) was added in solution containing 50 μL of TMB (1 mM) and 20 μL H2O2 of different concentration ranging from 0 μM to 200 μM in sodium acetate buffer (0.2 M) with pH set to 4.0, incubate for 35 min at 40 °C. For the electrochemical detection of H2O2, glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was modified with 10 μL of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite (0.2 μg/mL). Then Cyclic voltammetry was performed on developed electrode in phosphate buffer (pH 7) for the study of electrochemical response in presence of different concentration of H2O2 (Fig. S7).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization procedure of colorimetric sensing of glucose
ce:section.ce:para: Following procedure was adopted for colorimetric detection of glucose. 20 μL of GOx (5 mg/ml) was added into glucose solutions having different concentration in Phosphate buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.0), and incubated for 35 min at 40 °C to produce H2O2. Thereafter,10 μL of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite (0.2 μg/ml), 50 μL of TMB (1 mM) and 300 μL of sodium acetate buffer (0.2 M, pH 4.0) were added to the above mixture solution, and incubated for 35 min at 40 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of portable test kit for detection of diabetes in human using biological fluids (serum, saliva and tear)
ce:section.ce:para: We developed a portable test kit for checking glucose level in human biological fluids (serum, saliva and tear). All the samples were collected from healthy people (from our research group). Tear samples were collected by the use of onions through induced tearing and saliva samples were taken by psychological stimulation. Collected sample were boiled for denaturation of extra protein present in the sample and centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min. The serum samples were taken from healthy people with the help of our Institute hospital (IMS, BHU, Varanasi). For development of test kit, 20 mg agarose was dissolved in 2 ml of water and placed in hot plate to dissolve properly then agarose solution was allowed to cool up to approx 40 °C. Now, 50 μL of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite solution (10 μM), 50 μL GOx (5 mg/ml), 100 μL TMB (5 mM) and 100 μL of acetate buffer (0.2 M, pH 4.0) were added to this cooled agarose solution. After gentle mixing of the solution, 200 μL of the above solution was transferred to the wells of ELISA plate and left at room temperature for 5 min to make a gel like structure. This ELISA plate having a gel like structure into each of its well is our test kit, which we have developed and was stored at 4 °C. Analysis of the sensing performance of the system was done by adding 30 μL of serum/saliva/tear sample which was diluted 20 times in 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.0), to each well of the portable test kit and incubated for 30 min at 40 °C to permit the penetration of the reaction solution into the hydrogel. The hydrogel color was changed from colorless to bluish-green.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nanomaterials and their characterizations
ce:section.ce:para: The AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite obtained, is an urchin-like structure which is confirmed through UV–vis, XRD, zeta, AFM, HR-SEM, TEM, SAED pattern and EDS mapping. First we characterized MoS2-QDs by UV–vis spectra and observed the two absorption maxima peaks appearing at ∼247 nm and ∼357 nm which is a characteristic feature of MoS2-QDs shown in Fig. S2. The result suggests that optical absorption for low dimensional QDs exhibit a strong blue shift when the lateral dimensions reduces to <50 nm, ascribed to the quantum confinement.

The crystal structures of MoS2-QDs and AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite were investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements as shown in Fig. S3 (a and b). (002), (004), (101), (006), (106), (110), (108) faces are assigned to MoS2-QDs and the AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite has been assigned (002), (004), (101) due to MoS2-QDs and (111), (200), (220), (311) due to Au nanoparticles respectively (CAS no. 77-1716 and 7440-57-5).
AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite shows strong stability compared to MoS2-QDs was confirmed by ZETA potential graph (-10.5 mV for MoS2-QDs and −24.5 mV for AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite) in Fig. S3 (c and d).

The structural morphology of synthesized nanostructure has been investigated by TEM. Fig. 1 shows the uniform distribution of MoS2-QDs with an average size 2–4 nm. The inset of Fig. 1(a) shows the SAED pattern which suggests that MoS2-QDs is crystalline in nature.

TEM image shows MoS2-QDS nicely decorated over AuNPs to make urchin-like structure (Fig. 2 a–c). The HR-TEM image of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite (Fig. 2 d), shows the magnified image of the selected region, inside the yellow circle AuNPs have lattice fringe spacing ∼0.23 nm, and inside the red box MoS2-QDs layers are stacked with lattice fringe spacing ∼0.21 nm, which is in good agreement with the previous available reports [36]. We have the study of energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) mapping of metal-semiconductor composite of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs, from which one can infer about the presence of elemental composition such as sulphur, gold, molybdenum shown in Fig. S4.

In Fig. 3 , the surface topography of MoS2 QD and AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite has been investigated by AFM. Average thickness of MoS2 QDs is below 10 nm and after composite formation the thickness of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composites increases to ∼25 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite catalysis the oxidation of TMB as the peroxidase substrates
ce:section.ce:para: After successful synthesis, AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite system has been used to examine robust peroxidase mimetic activity through catalysis of TMB in the presence of H2O2 [37]. The catalyzed TMB shows bluish-green colored oxidation product yield to be directly proportional to the concentration of H2O2, which is showed as a typical absorbance peak at 652 nm. From Fig. 4 A, curve b, we observe that there is minor change in absorbance at 652 nm after the addition of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite to TMB along with extra peak appear at 450 nm due to diimine product of TMB. After the addition of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite and H2O2 to TMB solution, we found that the color of solution changed from colorless to bluish-green indicating TMB oxidation occurred, evident from the absorbance shown around 652 nm (curve c). As shown in Fig. 4B the typical absorbance peak intensity at 652 nm with respect to time of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite-TMB-H2O2 is higher and has faster catalytic activity than TMB–H2O2 and AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite–TMB system. Hence, it clearly indicates that AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite shows robust peroxidase mimetic activity towards the substrates.

We expected that the possible catalytic mechanism of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite came from enhancement of electron transfer leading to increase in electron density and mobility. In the current process, TMB molecules are adsorbed on the surface of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite and donate lone-pair electrons of the amino groups to AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite system. This results in an increase electron density and mobility of the AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite. The P-type nature of the semiconductor has a tendency to receive electrons from the TMB molecules having lone pair of electrons [38]. Due to the shift of electron density towards the composite, it confers the higher Fermi level and also the involvement of the excess electrons in the conduction band of AuNPs which results in an easy electron transfer to H2O2, leading to its faster breakdown into H2O and hydroxyl radicals catalyzed by AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite. The un-oxidized TMB combines with hydroxyl radicals to make charge transfer complex in the form of bluish green color which shows absorbance at around 350 nm and 652 nm (Fig. 4A). We have also studied the enzyme mimetic of different catalytic system (MoS2-QDs, AuNPs, simple mixture of AuNPs-MoS2-QDs and AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite) by absorption spectra based on TMB reaction. We found that our composite system shows higher catalytic activity than said catalytic materials. (Fig. S5)

The catalyzed TMB oxidation showed significant peaks at 365 nm and 652 nm, which were due to the formation of a charge transfer complex with un-oxidized TMB molecules in the form of blue colored product. We have used the developed sensing system for the analysis of H2O2 level which is corresponding to the change in color and absorbance intensity of TMB oxidation product at 652 nm.

The catalytic mechanism of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite in the presence of OH radical scavenger such as ascorbic acid, thiourea and O2 − scavenger i.e. Sodium Azide has been shown in Fig. S6. However, the absorbance intensity decreased in the case of ascorbic acid and thiourea as compared to blank, it also clearly shows that the OH radical scavengers. We perform the experiment to check the reducibility and OH radical scavengers properties of ascorbic acid [39]. Results suggested that TMB oxidation product blue color become fade after addition of ascorbic acid to prove reducibility (data not shown). However, after addition of TMB in ascorbic acid, H2O2, and AuNPs@MoS2-QDs reaction, there was no TMB oxidation occurs due to have OH radical scavengers. Further catalytic mechanism proved by thiourea which have OH radical scavengers properties. [40] Further, in case of Sodium Azide there was no change in the absorbance intensity, which indicates the presence of O2 − scavenger. Our results suggest that OH radicals are important for TMB oxidation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature, pH and H2O2 dependent AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite catalytic activity
ce:section.ce:para: We have studied the pH, Temperature and H2O2 dependent catalytic activity of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite shown in (Fig. 5 ). The activity of HRP is largely affected by the change in pH condition (lower to higher), temperature (above 60 °C) and H2O2 concentration. AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite shows good catalytic activity in wide range of temperature (25–80 °C) and pH (2.0–12.0) as compared to HRP (Fig. 5a, b).The optimum catalytic activity of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite was observed in acidic medium (pH 4.0) than other peroxidase mimetic system. The wide range of pH would be beneficial for sensing application. We have also checked the catalytic activity of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite system, which showed higher catalytic activity in wide range of H2O2 concentration in contrast to HRP as shown in (Fig. 5c). At optimum concentration the catalytic activity of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite is stable because there are no available active sites of the catalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Steady state kinetics of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite
ce:section.ce:para:
We investigated the steady state kinetics of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite system by keeping the concentration of TMB or H2O2 constant. We have calculated the initial rate (V0) of catalytic reaction by using the Beer–Lambert Law [11] equation.
(1) C = A/Ɛb

In this equation, C denotes substrate concentration, A is the absorbance; b is thickness of the solution. The TMB oxidation derived product concentration was calculated through the molar absorption coefficient Ɛ, having value 39,000 M−1 cm−1.[41] The AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite towards TMB and H2O2 used as substrate follows Michaelis–Menten equation at suitable concentrations. We have calculated the maximum initial velocity (Vmax) and Michaelis–Menten constant (Km) from the Michaelis–Menten equation (Fig. 6 a, b Eq. (2)).
(2)
V 0
=
V
m a x
[ S ]
K m
+
[ S ]

Where, V max denotes maximum initial velocity, Km is the substrate concentration (S) at (V max)/2. We have obtained higher V max of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite which reveals good catalytic activity towards TMB substrates. We obtained higher apparent Km value of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite with respect to H2O2 than HRP as shown in (Fig. 6b), from the experimental observation, higher concentration of H2O2 is needed for maximum activity. The apparent Km value of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite with TMB as substrates was found smaller than HRP Fig. 6(a). The results suggested that AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite shows higher affinity towards TMB substrates than HRP. We have compared the kinetic parameter of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite from literature and observed that our system showed good kinetics with low Km and higher V max than other catalytic nanomaterials because of higher surface area allowing more interaction between glucose oxidase and TMB molecules (table S2)

ce:section.ce:section-title: H2O2 detection based on AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite system by colorimetric and electrochemical
ce:section.ce:para: H2O2 detection based on the color variation of TMB oxidation catalyzed by AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite is shown in Fig. S7 a,b. The composite showed significant response in wide range of 0–200 μM of H2O2 with good linear relationship in 0.1 M PBS, (pH, 7.0). The linear regression equation obtained was A = 0.333 + 0.002C H2O2 with a correlation coefficient of 0.998. H2O2 detection was also carried out on the basis of electrochemical technique. Three electrode system, counter electrode of Pt plate, reference electrode Ag/AgCl and glassy carbon electrode modified with AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite as working electrode were taken and activity was measured in 0.1 M PBS, (pH, 7.0). The modified electrode was used for the study of catalytic activity of AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite in presence of H2O2 (Fig. S7 c,d), we observed a significant increase in current response when H2O2 was taken from 1 mM to 12 mM and the current response was linear with concentration.

The AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite system was used for the detection of glucose. We proposed sensing mechanism through schematic for colorimetric detection of glucose. The TMB substrates oxidized in the presence of H2O2 catalyzed by AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite to produce blue color. H2O2 is the indigenous product of redox reaction of GOx and glucose (Fig. 7 ).

Interference study is a useful method to check the selectivity of developed biosensor towards glucose detection based on AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite catalytic system. We have behavior of developed sensing system in the presence of interference. First we fixed the concentration of glucose (200 μM) with different interference consisting of ascorbic acid, cysteine, uric acid, urea, and cholesterol (5 mM). The results suggested that, the absorbance barely changed at 652 nm, indicating these interferences not aﬀ ;ect the colorimetric glucose sensor (Fig. 9 a). Ascorbic acid and cysteine have reducibility, however there was no interference was observed in glucose sensing which was reported in literature [48,49] It may be due to enzymatic oxidation product which minimize reduction of TMB oxidation. Furthermore, seen from Fig. 9b, we check excellent selectivity using the same interferences through colorimetric assay. The developed sensor shows good selectivity for glucose due to the specificity between GOx and glucose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of repeatability of biosensor system
ce:section.ce:para: We have validated the sensing experimental results through the study of reproducible test results of the developed system. We performed experiments for both intra (0–12 h.) and inter-day (0–3 days) of repeatability study under the same set of conditions for detection of glucose (1 μM to 400 μM) shown in Fig. 10 a and b. The findings suggested that the developed system (AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite) is reproducible and stable for detection of glucose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Testing the glucose level through portable test kit in human biological fluids (serum, saliva and tear)
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed biosensing system has been used for the detection of glucose in human biological fluids (serum, saliva and tear). Normal range of glucose in healthy person in serum, tear and saliva is 4–6 mM, 0.1–0.6 mM and 0.008–0.21 mM respectively but exceeding the concentration range causes various complications i.e. diabetes etc.

On the basis of our developed sensing platform based on AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite, we prepared an agarose hydrogel based stable visual platform in ELISA plate and checked the glucose level in serum samples by using this portable test kit. Observing the change in color and absorbance of agarose hydrogel provides a stable visual sensing of serum glucose. In the testing process, 20 μL of serum sample was diluted 20 times with phosphate buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.0) and glucose was added to get various known concentrations. From Fig. 11 , hydrogel color change from colorless (blank) to light blue/greenish along with absorbance at 652 nm revealing glucose in normal range but after increasing the concentration of glucose (as diabetic person), the color changes to dark blue/greenish color, which discloses higher level of glucose i.e. (8–12 mM).

The developed portable test kit also shows an excellent linearity with better detection limit for tear and saliva diluted samples. The technique is explored for detection of glucose in tear and saliva in order to prove the concept of proposed non-invasive detection method which shows an excellent detection of the glucose levels in wide range of concentration in tear and saliva samples as shown in Figs. 12 and 13
.

The AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite based biosensor system gave an excellent response for glucose detection in tear and saliva (Figs. 12 and 13). The developed portable test kit can be used for unaided eye detection of glucose without using any expensive instrumentation. We have also compared the glucose level in serum, saliva and tear samples by determining it with our developed method and conventional technique, Auto analyzer (based on enzymatic reaction). On the basis of results, we found that our proposed method is comparable to Auto analyzer for determination of glucose level (Table S3).

Summarizing, we have successfully synthesized the AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite which showed a robust peroxidase mimetic activity towards peroxidase substrates to produce colored product in the presence of H2O2. The AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite ideally follows the enzyme kinetics i.e. Michaelis–Menten kinetics with peroxidase mimetic activity against wide range of pH and temperature. It is believed that AuNPs@MoS2-QDs composite facilitates electron transfer for instantaneous breakdown of H2O2 into OH which combines with TMB to produce bluish-green color. We have utilized the composite system for successful detection of glucose level in buffer solution with excellent selectivity and sensitivity. The proposed method further is used to develop a portable test kit which is simple, inexpensive, for the detection of glucose level in human biological fluids (serum, saliva and tear). The designed bio-sensor is cable for noninvasive determination of glucose levels in tear and saliva. The developed colorimetric system based on peroxidase mimetic activity can be applicable in biotechnology and clinical diagnoses field.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Ms. Vinita and Dr. Narsingh R. Nirala convey their acknowledgement to the CSIR, JRF (File No- 09/013(0641)/2016-EMR-I) and SERB N-PDF (PDF/2016/000243) respectively. Authors are thankful to IMS, BHU hospital for providing bio-fluids and also to CIF, IIT (BHU) Varanasi for providing instrumentation facility.
All the experiments were performed and recorded by Ms. Vinita with Dr. Narsingh R. Nirala and responsible for the data.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.02.085.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Vinita received her master’s degeree in Chemistry from the Department of Chemistry, Institute of Science, Banaras Hindu University, Varanasi, India, in 2013. She is currently working for Ph.D. degree in the School of Materials Science and Technology as JRF. Her current research interest includes the development of metal-organic framework and organic/inorganic hybrid nanocomposites for sensing application.

Narsingh Raw Nirala received his Ph.D. degree from the Department of Zoology at Banaras Hindu University (BHU), India. He received his Master degree (M.Sc.) in Biotechnology from same University. At present he is PDF in the School of Materials Science and Technology, IIT (BHU) Varanasi. His current research interests focus on study of immobilization of biomolecules (enzyme and cancer biomarker) on different substrates (carbon materials and metal nanoparticles) for sensitive detection.

Rajiv Prakash received M. Tech. degree in Materials Science and Technology from School of Materials Science and Technology, IT-BHU in 1995 and Ph.D. degree in Chemistry, BHU, Varanasi, in the year 2000. Currently, he is Professor in the School of Materials Science and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology (BHU), Varanasi. He was Scientist in CSIR lab Lucknow, India for more than 7 years before joining IT-BHU. He has been recipients of the Young Scientist, Young Engineers (INAE) Awards of India and Materials Research Society of India Medal Award. His current research interests include synthesis of morphology-controlled organic conducting polymers, nanocomposites, fabrication and characterization of micro- and nano-organic electronic devices and sensors/biosensors. He has published more than 150 research papers in international journals of repute and having 16 patents to his credit out of which two technology transfers on portable sensor device.
